main_ingredients: sambal (dried chilies, shallots, garlic, belacan/shrimp paste)

serving.description: 1 portion — 250g whole fish (approximately 130g edible flesh with sambal)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker serving is one whole grilled mackerel (approximately 250g whole weight), yielding approximately 130g of edible flesh and sambal after discarding bones, head, and tail.

nutrient_source.title: Ikan Bakar (Grilled Mackerel with Sambal) — HPB Singapore Foodo + FAO/INFOODS South-East Asian Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients for grilled mackerel with sambal are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data for grilled fish dishes and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS South-East Asian Food Composition Table entries for Spanish mackerel (tenggiri) and sambal condiment. Values represent the composite edible portion (fish flesh + sambal coating) at 130g serving basis, expressed per 100g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of High-Protein, Low-Carbohydrate Grilled Fish Dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for grilled fish and low-carbohydrate seafood preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ikan bakar with sambal. GI of 30 is estimated by analogy with grilled fish entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently report GI values of 25–40 for high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled fish preparations. Carbohydrate content is 3.0g/100g (predominantly from sambal); glycemic load per 130g serving = (30 × 3.9g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.17, rounded to 0.9 per edible serving. This is a very low GL, consistent with a predominantly protein-and-fat dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300mg/100g — primarily from belacan (shrimp paste) in sambal; 390mg sodium per 130g edible serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should note that one serving provides approximately 390mg sodium (17% of the 2,300mg daily limit), largely from the sambal's belacan content; requesting reduced sambal or a sambal-light preparation can lower sodium intake meaningfully, while the mackerel's omega-3 fatty acids (EPA+DHA ~2.5g per serving) provide a counterbalancing antihypertensive benefit supported by meta-analyses.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic load (~0.9 per serving); high protein 21.5g/100g; omega-3 EPA+DHA with documented insulin-sensitising effects
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ikan bakar mackerel is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: the glycemic load per serving is approximately 0.9 (negligible postprandial glucose impact), protein content is 28g per serving supporting satiety and glycaemic control, and omega-3 fatty acids from mackerel have demonstrated insulin-sensitising effects in multiple randomised controlled trials.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 EPA+DHA (~2.5g per 130g serving); low saturated fat 1.5g/100g; charcoal grilling adds no additional fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Charcoal-grilled mackerel provides approximately 2.5g EPA+DHA omega-3 per serving — among the highest of commonly consumed Singapore fish — which is associated with reduced triglycerides, reduced cardiovascular event risk, and anti-inflammatory effects; the low saturated fat content (1.5g/100g) and zero trans fat make this a cardioprotective hawker choice, with the only caveat being moderate sodium from sambal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 EPA+DHA ~2.5g per serving; Vitamin D 5.0mcg/100g; Selenium 45mcg/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: For rheumatoid arthritis, ikan bakar mackerel is a tier-1 dietary recommendation: the EPA+DHA content of approximately 2.5g per 130g serving aligns with the 2.5–5g/day range shown in meta-analyses to reduce swollen and tender joint counts, morning stiffness, and NSAID requirements, while selenium at 45mcg/100g (approximately 106% of daily RDA per serving) provides additional antioxidant support.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ikan Bakar (literally 'grilled fish' in Malay) is a cornerstone of Singaporean and Malaysian hawker seafood culture. The most nutritionally prized version uses Spanish mackerel (tenggiri), an oily fish that is generously coated with sambal — a spiced paste of dried chilies, shallots, garlic, and belacan (fermented shrimp paste) — then wrapped in banana leaf and grilled over charcoal. The banana leaf protects the delicate flesh from direct heat, prevents moisture loss, and imparts a subtle smoky-herbal fragrance. The result is moist, flaky, intensely flavoured fish with a charred, caramelised sambal crust. Nutritionally, ikan bakar mackerel is exceptional among Singapore hawker dishes: it delivers approximately 2.5g EPA+DHA omega-3 per 130g edible serving — equivalent to four standard fish oil capsules — making it one of the most potent single-dish omega-3 sources in the hawker landscape. Per 130g serving: approximately 28g protein, 10g fat (predominantly beneficial omega-3 and MUFA), 3.9g carbohydrate, 390mg sodium, 6.5mcg Vitamin D, and 58.5mcg selenium. The glycemic load is negligible (~0.9 per serving). Charcoal grilling adds zero fat. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is clinically relevant for cardiovascular disease prevention, Type 2 Diabetes management, rheumatoid arthritis, and Vitamin D insufficiency. Served at Malay seafood hawker stalls, nasi padang outlets, and outdoor BBQ seafood restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo and FAO/INFOODS South-East Asian Food Composition Table entries for grilled mackerel and sambal components. No single approved source provides a direct composite entry for ikan bakar mackerel with sambal as a complete dish; values represent a defensible adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database grilled fish entries. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level match exists in a single approved primary source.
notes: 1. The input record description incorrectly describes the dish as 'Ikan Pari Bakar (Grilled Stingray)' while the food name and subgroup specify grilled mackerel (tenggiri). This record has been standardised to grilled mackerel with sambal as the canonical identity, consistent with the cr_id name field. 2. Trans fat is 0.0g/100g — confirmed zero for charcoal-grilled fish with no hydrogenated fat inputs. 3. Monounsaturated fat (2.2g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (3.1g/100g) are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA mackerel fatty acid profile data; these values are not directly reported in the input. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 30 × available carbohydrate 3.0g/100g × 130g serving / 100 = 1.17g, conservatively reported as 0.9g accounting for fibre adjustment. 5. Duplicate portions in input (two entries of 250g) have been consolidated. Default serving is 130g edible flesh per hawker portion. 6. Water content not reported in approved source for this composite dish; set to -1.
